questions.question: What does JDK stand for?
questions.answer: Java Development Kit

questions.question: What does the JRE provide?
options: A compiler to convert .java files into .class files
The runtime environment needed to run compiled Java programs
A text editor for writing Java source code
A debugger for stepping through Java programs
questions.answer: The runtime environment needed to run compiled Java programs

questions.question: Which of the following is true about the relationship between the JDK and the JRE?
options: The JDK and JRE are completely separate installations
The JDK contains the JRE plus development tools
The JDK replaces the JRE and they cannot coexist
questions.answer: The JDK contains the JRE plus development tools

questions.question: Which component does a developer need to compile and run Java programs?
questions.answer: The JDK

questions.question: What is the primary role of the JVM?
options: To compile Java source code into machine code
To execute Java bytecode on any supported platform
To edit and format Java source files
To package Java programs into JAR files
questions.answer: To execute Java bytecode on any supported platform

questions.question: Java's 'write once, run anywhere' capability is made possible by which component?
questions.answer: The Java Virtual Machine (JVM)

questions.question: What is the correct signature for the main method in a Java application?
questions.answer: public static void main(String[] args)

questions.question: In the main method signature, what does the keyword 'static' mean?
options: The method cannot be changed after compilation
The method belongs to the class itself, not to an instance
The method runs only once during the program's lifetime
The method is stored in a fixed memory location
questions.answer: The method belongs to the class itself, not to an instance

questions.question: In the main method signature, what does 'void' indicate?
options: The method is empty and contains no code
The method does not return a value
questions.answer: The method does not return a value

questions.question: What does 'String[] args' represent in the main method?
options: An array of command-line arguments passed to the program
A required set of configuration strings
The names of other methods in the class
questions.answer: An array of command-line arguments passed to the program

questions.question: What command is used to compile a Java source file named HelloWorld.java?
questions.answer: javac HelloWorld.java

questions.question: After compiling a Java source file, what type of file is produced?
questions.answer: A .class file containing bytecode

questions.question: What command runs a compiled Java program called MyApp?
questions.answer: java MyApp

questions.question: In Java, why must the file name match the public class name?
options: It is a convention but not enforced by the compiler
The Java compiler requires it and will produce an error if they do not match
It only matters when using packages
It is required by the operating system, not by Java
questions.answer: The Java compiler requires it and will produce an error if they do not match

questions.question: Given a file named Calculator.java, which class declaration is correct?
questions.answer: public class Calculator { }

questions.question: What happens if you omit a semicolon at the end of a System.out.println statement?
options: The program compiles but produces no output
The compiler automatically inserts the semicolon
questions.answer: A compile-time error occurs

questions.question: Which of the following is a compile-time error?
options: Accessing an array index that does not exist
Missing a closing curly brace in a class definition
Passing no command-line arguments when the program expects one
questions.answer: Missing a closing curly brace in a class definition

questions.question: Which of the following is a runtime error?
options: Forgetting a semicolon at the end of a statement
Accessing args[0] when no command-line arguments are provided
Using a lowercase 'm' instead of uppercase 'M' in the keyword 'main'
questions.answer: Accessing args[0] when no command-line arguments are provided

questions.question:
What is the output of the following code?

System.out.println("Result: " + (10 + 20));
questions.answer: Result: 30

questions.question: A Java program compiles successfully but does not run, producing the error 'Main method not found'. What is the most likely cause?
options: The file name does not match the class name
The main method is spelled with an uppercase 'M' as Main
The class is missing the public keyword
The program has no import statements
questions.answer: The main method is spelled with an uppercase 'M' as Main

questions.question: What does the keyword 'public' mean in the main method signature?
options: The method can only be called from within the same class
The method is accessible from anywhere
The method is visible only within the same package
The method can be overridden by subclasses
questions.answer: The method is accessible from anywhere

questions.question: How can you check the number of command-line arguments passed to a Java program?
questions.answer: args.length

questions.question: Which statement correctly prints the boolean value true to the console?
options: Both A and B produce the same output
Neither A nor B will compile
questions.answer: Both A and B produce the same output

questions.question: An end user who only wants to run Java applications (not develop them) needs which of the following?
options: An IDE such as IntelliJ or Eclipse
questions.answer: The JRE

questions.question: What is the minimum structure required for a valid Java application that can be compiled and run?
options: A class with any method defined inside it
A class with a public static void main(String[] args) method
A file containing only System.out.println statements
A class with a constructor and at least one field
questions.answer: A class with a public static void main(String[] args) method
